title: Synthetic Data Augmentation for LiDAR-Based HPS Estimation
problem: Current methods for Human Pose and Shape (HPS) estimation using LiDAR in uncontrolled environments suffer from limited real-world data, which lacks diversity in occlusion patterns and motion variations.
existing_methods: Existing approaches rely on sparse real-world LiDAR data, which is often insufficient to capture the full range of human motions and occlusions. These methods struggle with self-occlusions and external occlusions due to the view-dependent and sparsity-varying properties of LiDAR point clouds.
motivation: The scarcity and variability of real-world LiDAR data hinder the development of robust HPS models. Synthetic data can provide controlled variations in occlusions and motions, enabling better generalization.
hypothesis: Generating synthetic LiDAR data with controlled occlusions and human motions will improve the accuracy and robustness of HPS estimation models in uncontrolled environments.
method: We propose a synthetic data generation pipeline that combines parametric mesh models with LiDAR simulation to create diverse human motions and occlusion scenarios. The generated data will be used to augment real-world datasets, and a neural network trained on this augmented data will be evaluated for its ability to generalize to unseen environments.
evaluation: We will evaluate the proposed method on real-world datasets such as SLOPER4D and CIMI4D, using standard metrics like joint position error and shape reconstruction accuracy. We will compare our results against baselines that use only real-world data and measure improvements in performance under varying occlusion conditions.
significance: This approach addresses the data scarcity issue in LiDAR-based HPS estimation by leveraging synthetic data augmentation, leading to more robust and accurate models in uncontrolled environments This would provide a clearer research direction for Human Pose and Shape (HPS) estimation using LiDAR in uncontrolled environments.
caveats: The effectiveness of the synthetic data depends on the fidelity of the LiDAR simulation and the realism of the generated motions. Additionally, the method assumes that the synthetic data accurately represents real-world conditions.
